content.full_text:
CITY OF PLEASANTON
P.O. BOX 520 PLEASANTON, CALIFORNIA 94566-0802

DEC 28 1990

CITY OFFICES
123 MAIN STREET

CITY COUNCIL
484-8001

CITY MANAGER
484-8008

CITY ATTORNEY
484-8003

FINANCE
484-8033

PERSONNEL
484-8012

CITY OFFICES
200 OLD BERNAL AVE.

PLANNING
484-8023

ENGINEERING
484-8041

BUILDING INSPECTION
484-8015

COMMUNITY SERVICES
484-8360

WATER - BILLING
484-8038

FIELD SERVICES
333 SUNOL BLVD.

PARKS
484-8056

SANITARY SEWER
484-8061

STREET
484-8066

SUPPORT SERVICES
484-8067

WATER
484-8071

FIRE
4444 RAILROAD AVE.
484-8114

POLICE
4833 BERNAL AVE.
P.O. BOX 909
484-8127

December 28, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practice Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am writing to request an informal advice letter
concerning a possible conflict of interest of the Mayor
of the City of Pleasanton.

FACTS

Since November 12, 1990, Kenneth R. Mercer, the
elected Mayor of the City of Pleasanton, has been
employed by a title insurance company, Chicago Title
Insurance Incorporated, hereinafter "CTI." CTI is a
wholly-owned subsidiary of Allegheny International. The
stock of Allegheny International is traded on the New
York Stock Exchange; moreover, Allegheny International is
a Fortune 500 Company.

Mr. Mercer is a salaried employee of CTI and
receives no commissions. Although the full range of his
duties has not been defined, in general he will be
involved in marketing and public relations. Presumably
he will attempt to convince personnel from lending or
financial institutions, and major developers, to utilize
the escrow and title insurance services of CTI. His
primary responsibilities will be in Santa Clara, Contra
Costa and San Mateo Counties, and in the City and County
of San Francisco. He will not have assigned
responsibilities in Alameda County, where the City of
Pleasanton is located.

CTI has offices in many counties in California but
it does not do title insurance or escrow business in
Alameda County. In Alameda County, title insurance and
escrow business involving the name "Chicago Title" is

Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 2

_________________________

handled by a company called Chicago Title Insurance
Company of Alameda County, hereafter "Chicago Alameda."

CTI does not directly or indirectly own shares
possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of
Chicago Alameda; Chicago Alameda does not directly or
indirectly own shares possessing more than 50 percent of
the voting power of CTI. Moreover (a) neither entity has
a controlling ownership interest in the other, (b)
management and control is not shared between the
entities, and (c) a controlling owner in one is not a
controlling owner in the other. However, if CTI refers a
client to Chicago Alameda for Alameda County title
insurance or escrow business, CTI receives a referral
fee, totalling 30 percent, from Chicago Alameda.

ISSUE

Under what circumstances will Mr. Mercer's
employment with CTI cause him to be disqualified from
participating in decisions before the Pleasanton City
Council?

ANALYSIS

My analysis of this issue is set forth below for
whatever assistance it may be.

Government Code section 87100¹ prohibits a public
official from making, participating in, or using his
official position to influence a governmental decision in
which he knows he has reason to know he has a financial
interest. An official has a financial interest in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on, among other interests:

Any source of income, other than
gifts and other than loans by a

_________________________

¹All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2
California Administrative Code, §§18000, et seq. All references
to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California
Administrative Code.

Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 3

commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business on terms
available to the public without
regard to official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

Customers of CTI are not sources of income to Mr.
Mercer since he is not a 10 percent or greater owner of
the company and receives only a salary and no
commissions. However, CTI is a source of income to him
within the meaning of section 87103(c) and because he is
an employee of CTI under section 87103(d). Consequently,
he must disqualify himself from any decisions which will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial affect
on CTI which is distinguishable from the decisions'
effects on the public generally. Sections 87100 and
87103.

The effect of a decision is considered "reasonably
foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. Certainly it is not required; however, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is not "reasonably
foreseeable" (Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC Ops. 198 (No. 75-
089, Dec. 4, 1975).

In Thorner, the Commission discussed several
specific factual situations concerning the foreseeable
effect of a governmental decision when a company might
receive additional business as a result of the decision.
(Thorner, supra, at pp. 205-208.) With respect to
development projects, the FPPC has stated the following
circumstances would cause a city Council's decision on a
proposed development to foreseeably generate business for
a title company:

"(1) At the time of the
decision, the title insurance company
has entered into an agreement with
the developer to handle the title
insurance or escrow business for the
proposed development;


Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 4

_______________________


     (2) At the time of the
decision, the title insurance company
is negotiating an agreement with the
developer to handle the title
insurance or escrow business for the
proposed development; or

     (3) At the time of the
decision, the title insurance company
ordinarily handles the title
insurance or escrow business for the
developer's projects."

                              (O'Hara Advice Letter,
                              No. I-87-44, pages 3-4.)

     Because CTI does not do title or escrow business in
Alameda County, the circumstances under (1) or (2) above
would appear inapplicable. However, if CTI ordinarily
handles the title insurance and/or escrow business for a
developer, and if that developer has a project before the
Pleasanton City Council, then the provisions of (3) would
appear to be applicable. In that circumstance, Mr.
Mercer would be disqualified from participating in the
decision if the effect on CTI would be material, as
discussed below.

     Because of the similarity in the names of CTI and
Chicago Alameda, and because at the time of a Council
decision Chicago Alameda could have an agreement with the
applicant to handle the title insurance or escrow
business for the proposed development, would that fact
foreseeably generate business for CTI triggering his
disqualification?

     Section 18706 of the Regulations provides that an
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on a business entity which is a
parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a
business entity in which the official has a financial
interest.

     A parent-subsidiary exists when one corporation
directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than
50 percent of the voting power of another corporation.
Section 18236(a) of the Regulations. CTI has no parent-


Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 5

_________________________


subsidiary relationship with Chicago Alameda based on the
above-mentioned test.

Business entities which do not have a parent-
subsidiary relationship are "otherwise related" if any
one of the following three tests is met:

(1) One business entity has a controlling
ownership interest in the other business
entity.

(2) There is shared management and
control between the entities. In determining
whether there is shared management and control,
consideration should be given to the following
factors:

(A) The same person or
substantially the same person owns
and manages the two entities;

(B) There are common or
commingled funds or assets;

(C) The business entities share
the use of the same offices or
employees, or otherwise share
activities, resources or personnel on
a regular basis;

(D) There is otherwise a
regular and close working
relationship between the entities; or

(3) A controlling owner (50 percent or
greater interest as a shareholder or as a
general partner) in one entity also is a
controlling owner in the other entity.

Section 18236(b) of
the Regulations.

Based on these tests, CTI is not otherwise related to
Chicago Alameda.

Notwithstanding that CTI is not related to Chicago
Alameda, there is one situation involving CTI and Chicago


Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 6

_________________________


Alameda which would reasonably generate business for CTI.
If CTI refers a client to Chicago Alameda for Alameda
County title insurance or escrow business, CTI receives a
referral fee, totaling 30 percent, from Chicago Alameda.
Should this referred client be involved in a matter
before the City Council, the foreseeable portion of the
test would be met and Mr. Mercer would be disqualified
from participating in the decision if the effect on CTI
would be material.

Regulation 18702.2 contains guidelines for
determining materiality. These guidelines vary with the
financial size of the business entity. Because Allegheny
International, which owns CTI is traded on the New York
Stock Exchange, and is a Fortune 500 Company, the effect
of the decision will be material if the decision (1) will
result in an increase or decrease to the company's gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $1,000,000 or more or (2)
will result in the company's incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in an amount of $250,000 or
more; or (3) will result in the company's increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$1,000,000 or more. See §18702.2(a)(1).

CONCLUSION

Due to CTI's lack of business in Alameda County and
because the threshold amounts are extremely high, a
conflict of interest should not ordinarily arise in
context of Mr. Mercer's decisions as the Mayor of
Pleasanton. In most cases, consequently, I conclude that
Mr. Mercer's employment with CTI will not create a
conflict of interest vis-a-vis his seat on the City
Council.

I would appreciate your comments on this analysis
and your specific advice as to when Mr. Mercer's
employment with CTI will result in Mr. Mercer's being
disqualified from participating in decisions before the
Pleasanton City Council.

Scott Hallabrin
December 28, 1990
Page 7

_________________________


If you have any questions or need further
information, please feel free to contact me.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

MICHAEL H. ROUSH
City Attorney

MHR:wd
cc: Kenneth R. Mercer, Mayor

sections.question_synthetic: The City of Pleasanton's city attorney seeks FPPC advice on when Mayor Kenneth R. Mercer's employment with Chicago Title Insurance Company (CTI) would create a conflict of interest requiring his disqualification from city council decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a city attorney to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. The document includes a detailed ANALYSIS section containing the requester's legal analysis and interpretation of relevant law, but this is not an FPPC conclusion. The letter explicitly requests FPPC comments and advice. Document dated December 28, 1990; document ID appears to be UNK-91-10261 indicating FPPC's internal filing.

embedding.summary: City of Pleasanton city attorney Michael H. Roush requests an informal FPPC advice letter regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from Mayor Kenneth R. Mercer's employment with Chicago Title Insurance Company, and provides a detailed analysis of applicable conflict of interest provisions and regulations.
